action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: farmer, ship, stone, hill, dog, boat, lake, road, river, hunter, spear, bird, tree, king
Adjectives: old, dark, tall, big, blue, small, cold, red
Verbs (write them in this base form): see, watch, hold, carry, find, hear, chase, feed
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three, four, five, six (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three, four, five, six) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
- zero or more adjectives may come before the noun.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a farmer see a ship
    six farmers see a ship
    a old farmer see a ship

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    the two big roads hear the dog  =>  tuko fidutofato kagugu teki bobato
    the six hunters carry four red hills  =>  befe tafipafato pobo ba gigofa bade
    four hills feed the five ships  =>  ba gigofa bogago bi fadafato
    the five red farmers chase a small tree  =>  bi botafato bade pofo toki gidido
    the four trees watch the old king  =>  ba tokifato gufaki kagito bugu
    the river watch the two dark birds  =>  figaketo gufaki tuko dufifato defe
    the five red trees watch the two cold trees  =>  bi tokifato bade gufaki tuko tokifato gida
    the two small hunters hear the three lakes  =>  tuko tafipafato gidido teki guba gagofato
    six old stones feed the six rivers  =>  befe bukadafa bugu bogago befe figakefato
    a blue road watch a cold hunter  =>  fiduto kabage gufaki tafipa gida
    the cold ship find a small boat  =>  fadato gida piki degogo gidido
    five big boats see a tall spear  =>  bi degogofa kagugu peke kuko fubu
    six small hunters hold a tree  =>  befe tafipafa gidido kipi toki

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 5 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 4 English sentences (so at most
   20 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 4 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to systematically discover the morphology and syntax of this language. From the examples, I can see patterns like "-fato" appearing frequently, "teki" and "kagugu" as potential markers, and various prefixes. Let me start by testing basic structures and word correspondences.

PROBES:
1. a farmer see a ship
2. a stone see a hill
3. a dog see a bird
4. a hunter see a tree
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a farmer see a ship"  =>  bota peke fada
2. "a stone see a hill"  =>  bukada peke gigo
3. "a dog see a bird"  =>  boba peke dufi
4. "a hunter see a tree"  =>  tafipa peke toki
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 5 done. Probes used: 4 of 20. 4 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I can map basic word forms. From these results: bota=farmer, bukada=stone, boba=dog, dufi=bird, gigo=hill, toki=tree, tafipa=hunter, fada=ship, peke=see. I also notice -ta, -da, -da, -pi, -go, -ki, -pi, -da patterns. Let me test plural forms and other verbs to understand the morphology system.

PROBES:
1. two farmers see a ship
2. a farmer see two ships
3. a farmer hear a ship
4. a farmer hold a ship
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "two farmers see a ship"  =>  tuko botafa peke fada
2. "a farmer see two ships"  =>  bota peke tuko fadafa
3. "a farmer hear a ship"  =>  bota teki fada
4. "a farmer hold a ship"  =>  bota kipi fada
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 5 done. Probes used: 8 of 20. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I see clear patterns: -fa suffix marks plural (botafa, fadafa), -ta for farmer, -da for stone/ship, -pi for hunter/hold. Also teki=hear, kipi=hold. Let me test more verbs and check if plural markers work consistently, and test adjectives.

PROBES:
1. a farmer chase a ship
2. a farmer feed a ship
3. a farmer watch a ship
4. a farmer carry a ship
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a farmer chase a ship"  =>  bota pofo fada
2. "a farmer feed a ship"  =>  bota bogago fada
3. "a farmer watch a ship"  =>  bota gufaki fada
4. "a farmer carry a ship"  =>  bota pobo fada
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 5 done. Probes used: 12 of 20. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I have all verbs: peke=see, teki=hear, kipi=hold, pofo=chase, bogago=feed, gufaki=watch, pobo=carry. I need to test adjectives and definiteness markers. From earlier examples, I saw "teki" might be a definite marker and various adjective forms. Let me test these systematically.

PROBES:
1. the farmer see a ship
2. a old farmer see a ship
3. a big farmer see a ship
4. a blue farmer see a ship
action.label: response

probes: a old farmer see a ship
a big farmer see a ship
a blue farmer see a ship

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the farmer see a ship"  =>  botato peke fada
2. "a old farmer see a ship"  =>  bota bugu peke fada
3. "a big farmer see a ship"  =>  bota kagugu peke fada
4. "a blue farmer see a ship"  =>  bota kabage peke fada
STATUS: investigation round 4 of 5 done. Probes used: 16 of 20. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I have the key patterns: -to suffix marks definite (botato), and adjectives: bugu=old, kagugu=big, kabage=blue. I need to test a few more adjectives and verify the plural+definite combination, and test a couple more nouns to ensure I have the complete mapping.

PROBES:
1. the two farmers see a ship
2. a small farmer see a ship
3. a red farmer see a ship
4. a cold farmer see a ship
action.label: response

probes: the two farmers see a ship
a small farmer see a ship
a red farmer see a ship
a cold farmer see a ship

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the two farmers see a ship"  =>  tuko botafato peke fada
2. "a small farmer see a ship"  =>  bota gidido peke fada
3. "a red farmer see a ship"  =>  bota bade peke fada
4. "a cold farmer see a ship"  =>  bota gida peke fada

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. two tall rivers chase two big dogs
2. five cold hills chase five small kings
3. the four red roads hold six kings
4. three lakes carry the six hunters
5. a tree hold the tall river
6. the two red hills watch the four cold dogs
7. the four tall birds feed the two ships
8. the farmer hold the five dogs
9. four birds see two spears
10. the two lakes watch three small spears

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Based on my investigation, I now have the complete grammar:
- Nouns: bota=farmer, fada=ship, bukada=stone, gigo=hill, boba=dog, degogo=boat, gagogo=lake, fiduto=road, figaketo=river, tafipa=hunter, fubu=spear, dufi=bird, toki=tree, bugu=king
- Adjectives: bugu=old, dufi=dark, kuko=tall, kagugu=big, kabage=blue, gidido=small, gida=cold, bade=red
- Verbs: peke=see, gufaki=watch, kipi=hold, pobo=carry, bogago=feed, teki=hear, pofo=chase
- Definite: -to suffix (botato, fadato, etc.)
- Plural: -fa suffix (botafa, fadafa, etc.)
- Definite plural: -fato suffix (botafato, fadafato, etc.)
- Number words: tuko=two, guba=three, ba=four, bi=five, befe=six
- Word order: SUBJECT verb OBJECT

ANSWERS:
1. tuko figaketofa kuko pofo tuko bobafa kagugu
2. bi gigofa gida pofo bi bugofa gidido
3. ba fidutofato bade kipi befe bugufato
4. guba gagogofa pobo befe tafipafato
5. toki kipi figaketo kuko
6. tuko gigofato bade gufaki ba bobafato gida
7. ba dufifato kuko bogago tuko fadafato
8. botato kipi bi bobafato
9. ba dufifa peke tuko fubafa
10. tuko gagogofato gufaki guba fubafa gidido
action.label: response

answers: tuko figaketofa kuko pofo tuko bobafa kagugu
bi gigofa gida pofo bi bugofa gidido
ba fidutofato bade kipi befe bugufato
tuko gigofato bade gufaki ba bobafato gida
ba dufifato kuko bogago tuko fadafato
tuko gagogofato gufaki guba fubafa gidido
targets: tuko figakefa fubu pofo tuko bobafa kagugu
bi gigofa gida pofo bi kagifa gidido
ba fidutofato bade kipi befe kagifa
tuko gigofato bade gufaki ba bobafato gida
ba dufifato fubu bogago tuko fadafato
tuko gagofato gufaki guba kukofa gidido
